region_of_origin: Singapore (served at Khao - Funan; generic preparation with broad regional roots)
food_subgroup: Roasted / baked whole fish or fish fillet

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03302051 covers baked fish food products; used as closest parent as no specific roasted fish code exists. Roasting and baking are closely related dry-heat methods.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Fish, cooked) is the closest defensible parent category. No specific roasted fish sub-code identified in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226888001 (Cooked fish) is the closest parent. No roasted-fish-specific SNOMED code confirmed.

main_ingredients: white fish fillet or whole fish (e.g. snapper, barramundi, tilapia, or similar)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of roasted fish is approximately 150 grams, equivalent to one medium fillet or a modest portion of a whole roasted fish as served in a Singapore hawker or healthy-eating restaurant context.

nutrient_source.title: Fish, mixed species, cooked, dry heat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 15264 / FDC ID 175168)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No Singapore-specific roasted fish entry found in HPB Singapore Foodo for a generic roasted fish preparation. USDA SR Legacy 'Fish, mixed species, cooked, dry heat' is used as the closest defensible analogue for a lean white fish roasted without heavy saucing. Values represent a typical lean white fish (e.g. snapper, barramundi, tilapia) cooked by dry heat. Sodium adjusted upward slightly to reflect typical seasoning in a restaurant context (estimated 350 mg/100 g). All other macros and micronutrients are taken directly from the USDA SR Legacy entry.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Foods: A Physiological Basis for Carbohydrate Exchange — Jenkins et al. (1981); and University of Sydney GI Database general principle: pure protein and fat foods have a GI of effectively zero
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: Roasted fish contains negligible carbohydrate (0.0 g per 100 g) and therefore has no measurable glycemic response. The University of Sydney GI Database and established nutritional science confirm that foods with no digestible carbohydrate do not raise blood glucose. Per the Veritas zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule adapted for zero-carbohydrate foods, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive value) to meet scoring model requirements for a numeric GI > 0; gi_category is 'low' and glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate_g per serving is 0.0 g.

hypertension.guidance: Roasted fish as prepared in restaurant settings may contain approximately 350 mg sodium per 100 g due to added salt and marinades; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion size to manage daily sodium intake below 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content and high-quality protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: Roasted fish is an excellent protein source with effectively zero carbohydrate (0.0 g per 100 g), producing no glycemic response, making it a highly suitable protein choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when paired with low-GI vegetables and whole grains.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean protein with low saturated fat and beneficial omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Roasted fish provides lean protein with low saturated fat (approximately 0.6 g per 100 g) and contains omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids that support cardiovascular health; dry-heat roasting without heavy oil or butter preserves the favourable lipid profile, making it a heart-healthy protein option.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Roasted fish is a minimally processed, high-protein dish consisting of a whole fish or fish fillet — typically a lean white fish such as snapper, barramundi, tilapia, or sea bass — cooked by dry-heat roasting in an oven or over an open flame. The preparation involves light seasoning with salt, herbs, and spices, and a minimal coating of oil to prevent sticking and promote browning. The result is a firm, flaky texture with a lightly caramelised exterior and a moist, tender interior. In the Singapore healthy-eating restaurant context, roasted fish is served as a main protein component in bento boxes, grain bowls, or salad plates, consistent with the cuisine tags 'Healthy' and 'Bento' associated with this dish at Khao - Funan, Funan Mall. Nutritionally, roasted fish is an excellent source of complete protein (approximately 26 g per 100 g), B vitamins including B12 and niacin, selenium, phosphorus, and vitamin D. It contains negligible carbohydrate and low saturated fat, making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and weight. The primary nutritional concern is sodium from seasoning, which may be elevated in restaurant preparations. Roasted fish is naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal-compatible when prepared without pork-derived ingredients.
common_user_queries: How many calories in roasted fish?
Is roasted fish good for diabetics?
Roasted fish vs grilled fish nutrition
Can I eat roasted fish if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact Singapore-specific roasted fish entry was found in HPB Singapore Foodo for a generic preparation. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy 'Fish, mixed species, cooked, dry heat', which is a defensible analogue for a lean white fish prepared by dry heat. Sodium has been adjusted upward to reflect typical restaurant seasoning. Confidence is medium rather than high because the exact fish species and preparation at Khao - Funan are not specified in the input record, and no direct HPB or ICMR-NIN match was available.
notes: 1. carbohydrate_g is 0.0 g per 100 g: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for fish cooked by dry heat with no carbohydrate-containing ingredients. This is naturally zero for plain roasted fish. sugar_g is 0.0: naturally zero as fish contains no sugar. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: naturally zero as fish contains no dietary fibre. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for cooked fish. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for lean fish cooked by dry heat. 2. glycemic_index is set to 1 (effectively zero) per Veritas v2 scoring model requirements for zero-carbohydrate foods; gi_category is 'low' and glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 per convention for zero-carbohydrate foods. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.8), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.7), and all micronutrient values are sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 175168. 4. Sodium_mg is estimated at 350 mg/100 g to reflect typical restaurant seasoning; the USDA SR Legacy base value for unseasoned cooked fish is approximately 75–90 mg/100 g. 5. The restaurant 'Khao - Funan' is a Thai-inspired healthy-eating concept at Funan Mall, Singapore; the dish is listed under 'Healthy' cuisine. 6. No -1 sentinel values are used for fields where USDA SR Legacy provides data. Fields retaining -1 are not applicable or not reported in the source: none in this record — all fields have been populated with source values or confirmed zeros. 7. FoodOn FOODON_03302051, FoodEx2 A00LC, and SNOMED CT 226888001 are used as closest_parent matches; no exact roasted-fish-specific codes were identified in any of the three ontology systems.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected glycemic_index from 0 to 1, as required by the Veritas v2 scoring model for zero-carbohydrate foods to be a numeric value greater than zero. Updated gi_source.source_match_notes and the main notes section to reflect and justify this change.
Populated all mandatory multilingual local_names for Singapore-served dish: Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi.
Sourced nutrient values from USDA SR Legacy FDC 175168 as closest defensible analogue; documented adaptation in nutrient_source.
Adjusted sodium upward to 350 mg/100 g to reflect restaurant seasoning context.
Documented all zero values (carbohydrate_g, sugar_g, dietary_fibre_g, vitamin_c_mg, trans_fat_g) with 'naturally zero' or 'confirmed zero' justifications in notes.
Provided three ontology anchors using only allowed match_type values (closest_parent); included gap notes for all.
Ensured aliases_by_language and flat aliases are perfectly consistent — all strings verified verbatim.
Wrote natural_language_description exceeding 200 characters with full clinical and nutritional context.
Included all three health conditions with specific clinical guidance; used 'Type 2 Diabetes' as human_condition_name.
